summary:
The Roman census was one of the Republic's most fundamental institutions, conducted every five years by the censors, who enumerated all Roman citizens, registered their property and family status, assessed their tax liabilities, and assigned them to their appropriate class in the tribal and centuriate assemblies. The census served simultaneously as a population count, property register, tax assessment, military draft roll, and moral audit of Roman society.

Censors also exercised the censoria nota — the power to add a mark of censure next to a citizen's name for moral failings, which could result in downgrading to an inferior voting class or, for senators, removal from the Senate rolls. This moral oversight dimension meant the census was not merely administrative but a statement of Roman values. Census records show Roman citizen population growing from roughly 300,000 in the 3rd century BC to over 900,000 by the time of Julius Caesar's late Republic census reforms. The data has been debated by modern historians — some argue the figures count only adult male citizens, others that they include all free citizens. The Roman census was the most sophisticated population-counting institution of the ancient world and a model for later administrative states.
